nativeRange.habitat: Rapids, riffles, and fast-moving shallow streams with large smooth boulders and cobbles; also documented in deeper stream pools. Substrate primarily rock and gravel with little sediment. Water is cool, highly oxygenated, and clear.

decorPreferences: smooth river rocks and rounded cobbles
flat slate or flagstone pieces for resting
powerhead or wavemaker for strong directional flow
minimal fine substrate (sand or fine gravel acceptable)

diet.notes: Primarily grazes biofilm, aufwuchs (periphyton), soft green algae, and diatoms from rock surfaces. Cannot subsist on algae wafers alone — mature biofilm from seasoned rockwork is essential. Will accept sinking algae wafers, Repashy gel foods (Soilent Green or similar), blanched zucchini or cucumber, and frozen spirulina brine shrimp. Has also been documented eating small benthic invertebrates; some sources classify it as omnivorous, but plant material and biofilm dominate its natural diet. May decline and starve in a new tank lacking established biofilm.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (danios, rasboras, white cloud mountain minnows)
other peaceful community fish tolerant of cool, high-flow conditions
avoidWith: other hillstream loach species of similar body shape (territorial competition)
slow-water species unable to cope with strong flow

summary: Striking hillstream specialist from Vietnamese mountain streams; requires cool water, powerful flow, and mature biofilm to thrive. A beautiful but specialized fish best kept in a dedicated hillstream biotope.
careNotes:
The Reticulated Hillstream Loach is a highly specialized hillstream fish that demands dedicated setup conditions — it generally does not thrive in a standard community tank. The three non-negotiable requirements are: (1) cool water temperature kept consistently between 20–24°C (temperatures above 24°C have been reported to cause rapid decline; a chiller or cool room may be necessary in warm climates), (2) strong, high-volume water flow replicating rapids (a turnover of 15–20× per hour is commonly cited; powerheads or wavemakers pointed across smooth rock surfaces are typical), and (3) mature biofilm established on rocks — this is the primary food source and takes weeks to develop in a new tank.

Family note: Sewellia lineolata is classified in family Gastromyzontidae (not Balitoridae as some older sources state — Balitoridae was split and Gastromyzontidae erected for the Southeast Asian hillstream loaches). FishBase and SeriouslyFish both confirm Gastromyzontidae.

The species is generally peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates, but males are somewhat territorial with each other. Groups of 3 or more are commonly recommended; SeriouslyFish suggests 6+ for best natural behavior. Avoid keeping pairs, as one individual may bully the other. Other hillstream loach genera (Gastromyzon, Hypergastromyzon) may compete for the same streamlined substrate territories.

These fish have a flattened, ray-like body with enlarged pectoral and pelvic fins allowing them to grip smooth rock surfaces in strong current. They are accomplished climbers and have been known to exit tanks through small gaps — a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised.

Biofilm is critical: a new tank will not support these fish well. Running the tank for 6–8 weeks before adding Sewellia, with rock surfaces exposed to light, is generally advised to grow sufficient periphyton. Once established, supplement with algae wafers, gel foods, and blanched vegetables. The species is IUCN Vulnerable (VU) as of 2010; most trade specimens are wild-collected, so supporting captive-bred stock where available is encouraged.

Copper sensitivity: as with many Gastromyzontidae, copper-based medications should be avoided; use copper-free treatments instead.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has been documented and is considered achievable with patience. SeriouslyFish notes spawning is triggered by a slight temperature increase to approximately 25–26°C or by cool water changes. Eggs are deposited in gravel substrate interstices and receive no parental care. Fry consume infusoria initially, then accept Artemia nauplii and microworms as they grow. A mature tank with significant mulm and biofilm in the substrate supports fry survival. Aquarium Co-Op notes fry reach roughly 5 mm before adults stop treating them as prey.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Primary cross-check source. Confirms temperature 20–24°C, pH 6.0–7.5, adult size 55–65 mm, family Gastromyzontidae, order Cypriniformes, native range Vietnam (several provinces) + Laos. Recommends group of 6+; used 3 as minimum floor (Co-Op and Aquatic Arts both cite 3+). Hardness given as 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dGH); used gH 5–15 and kH 4–12 per broader source consensus and user key data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirms family Gastromyzontidae, order Cypriniformes, max size 5.7 cm SL, native to China/Vietnam/Cambodia (Mekong basin). IUCN Vulnerable (VU). Used as taxonomy and size cross-check.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Confirms temperature 68–75°F (20–24°C), pH 6.5–7.5, group of 3+, fast-moving water requirement. Consistent with SeriouslyFish on temperature ceiling. Used as third source confirming cool-water specialist profile.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning triggered by temperature rise to 25–26°C or cool water changes; eggs deposited in gravel substrate. Fry initially consume infusoria. Achievable with a mature, biofilm-rich setup.
